% =====================================================================
% DRAFT: Compressed replacement for Sections 5.4--5.7 and Interpretation
%
% Sections 5.1 (error budget), 5.2 (continuous scale), 5.3 (alternative
% scales) remain unchanged.  Everything below replaces 5.4 onward.
% =====================================================================

\subsection{Degree-of-freedom accounting and null-model context}
\label{sec:dof}

Before reporting Monte Carlo checks, it is useful to state explicitly
which aspects of the comparison were selected by the author and which
were determined by the construction.

\paragraph{Determined quantities (no selection freedom).}
The input triple $(e,\mu,\tau)$ is inherited from the Koide
relation~(\ref{eq:koide}); no search over triples was performed.
Given a Descartes reading of that triple, the two Soddy curvatures
are determined by the three input curvatures; the choice between
inner and outer root is a binary selection (one bit).
The comparison exponent $n=2$ is fixed by dimensional consistency:
$\mathcal{F}$ has units of $\text{MeV}^{1/2}$, so only
$\mathcal{F}^2$ carries mass dimension one.
The evaluation scale $\mu_\star = \sum m_\ell$ is the quantity that
already appears in the numerator of the Koide
relation~(\ref{eq:koide}); it is not an independently tuned parameter.
All signs are positive, again inherited from the established
charged-lepton Koide relation.

\paragraph{Genuine selection freedom.}
The comparison target is one of six quark species; this is the
principal source of look-elsewhere effect.  As shown in
Table~\ref{tab:loose}, among all five light quarks only the strange
quark produces a hit at the stated tolerance, and the charm and
bottom masses are too far from $\mathcal{F}^2 \approx 95$~MeV at
any perturbative scale for the comparison to be close.

\paragraph{Contrast with unconstrained Koide searches.}
Three-point Koide closures of the form $Q(m_1, m_2, m_3) \approx 2/3$
become common once one admits a search over quark triples
($\binom{6}{3} = 20$ candidates), sign assignments ($\pm$ on each
square root, four patterns), and running evaluation scales (multiple
physically motivated scales per quark).  In an audited scan of the
Standard Model quark spectrum over this full search space---20
triples $\times$ 4 sign patterns $\times$ a discrete list of
physically interpretable evaluation scales per
quark---approximately half of all random spectra produce at least one
close three-point closure.  The mechanism is straightforward: each
additional search knob multiplies the number of independent trials,
and the accumulated look-elsewhere effect overwhelms any individual
match.  The Descartes/Soddy construction moves in the opposite
direction: it takes one established triple, determines a fourth
quantity from it, and inherits its scale from the same expression.

\paragraph{Null-model Monte Carlo.}
To quantify the rarity of the joint condition (Koide saturation plus
Soddy--quark match), we sample Koide-conditioned triples as follows.
Draw $m_3$ log-uniformly from $[10^0,10^4]$~MeV and $m_1$
log-uniformly from $[10^{-3},10^{-1}]\cdot m_3$; solve $m_2$
analytically from $Q(m_1,m_2,m_3) = 2/3$.  Retain only triples with
$\mu_\star > 1$~GeV (perturbative running regime), accumulating
$25{,}485$ valid triples.  For each, compute $\mathcal{F}^{(i)}$ and
$\mu_\star^{(i)}$, run $m_s^{\msbar}(\mu_\star^{(i)})$ from the
FLAG 2~GeV input, and record a hit if
$|\mathcal{F}^{(i)\,2} - m_s^{\msbar}(\mu_\star^{(i)})| <
\sigma_{m_s}(\mu_\star^{(i)})$.  Of the retained triples, $121$
produce hits:
\begin{equation}
p_{\text{tight}} = 0.47\%,\qquad
95\%\;\text{Clopper--Pearson CI:}\;[0.39\%,\,0.57\%].
\label{eq:tight_null_v2}
\end{equation}
Broadening the comparison to all five quark species adds no
additional hits (Table~\ref{tab:loose}): charm, bottom, up, and down
masses are too far from $\mathcal{F}^2$ at any perturbative scale.
The hit fraction is stable across four prior choices (range
$0.23\%$--$0.47\%$, fold variation $2.0$; see
Table~\ref{tab:priors}) and three nontrivial lower-cutoff choices
(range $0.41\%$--$0.58\%$, fold variation $1.4$).

These are conditional frequencies under specific Monte Carlo
constructions, not $p$-values.  Their purpose is limited: they
confirm that the Descartes/Soddy match is not explained by the same
look-elsewhere mechanism that makes unconstrained three-point
running-scale Koide searches uninformative.  We draw no further
inferential conclusion from them.


% =====================================================================
% SUPPLEMENTARY NOTE: Look-Elsewhere Context
% (standalone supplement, can be appended or included separately)
% =====================================================================

\newpage
\section*{Supplementary Note: Look-Elsewhere Context}

The main text reports a post-hoc numerical observation:
\[
\mathcal{F}^2 = 95.113~\text{MeV}
\;\approx\;
m_s^{\msbar}(\mu_\star),
\qquad
\mu_\star = m_e + m_\mu + m_\tau.
\]
Because Koide-type mass relations are vulnerable to look-elsewhere
effects, we include here a limited Monte Carlo context calculation.
The purpose is not to assign an objective probability to the
observation.  The purpose is narrower: to compare the observed
Descartes/Soddy construction with the known failure mode of
three-point Koide searches, namely the inflation of apparent
significance when running-scale freedom is admitted.

\paragraph{Three-point Koide searches.}
For a three-mass Koide expression
\[
Q(m_1,m_2,m_3)
= \frac{m_1 + m_2 + m_3}
{\bigl(s_1\sqrt{m_1} + s_2\sqrt{m_2} + s_3\sqrt{m_3}\bigr)^2},
\qquad s_i \in \{\pm 1\},
\]
close numerical closures become common once one allows a search over
triples, signs, and running evaluation scales.  In an audited
quark-only scan over $20$ triples, four sign assignments, and a
discrete list of physically interpretable evaluation scales, the
probability of at least one close three-point closure in a random
spectrum approaches $50\%$.  This is the overfitting cliff: once the
running scale becomes a search knob, a close three-point closure is
no longer surprising.

The cliff is visible even in a controlled ablation.  Starting from
a single constrained triple evaluated at a single physically
motivated scale (hit rate ${\sim}0.5\%$), each additional degree of
freedom---admitting sign flips, admitting per-particle scale
choices---raises the hit rate monotonically.  By the time all three
particles in a triple are free to run independently, the hit rate
exceeds that of the constrained starting point by an order of
magnitude.  When the search is broadened to all $20$ quark triples
with full sign and scale freedom, approximately half of random
spectra contain at least one close closure.

This observation is not used as evidence against every possible quark
Koide relation.  It only says that broad three-point running-scale
searches are insufficiently constrained to be informative.

\paragraph{The four-point Descartes/Soddy construction.}
The construction in the main text is different.  Once the
charged-lepton Koide triple is interpreted through the ordinary
Descartes circle theorem, the fourth curvature is not freely chosen.
The two Descartes roots are fixed by the three input curvatures
$k_i = \sqrt{m_i}$.  Choosing the outer root gives
\[
\mathcal{F} = k_4^- = e_1 - 2\sqrt{e_2},
\]
and dimensional consistency fixes the comparison quantity to
$\mathcal{F}^2$.  The evaluation scale $\mu_\star = \sum m_\ell$ is
the quantity already present in the Koide numerator, not an
independently tuned parameter.  The remaining numerical comparison
is then whether this derived fourth mass-scale quantity lies near a
known quark mass at a stated scale.

Thus the Descartes/Soddy comparison is a four-point constraint
rather than a three-point closure.  Each element that inflates the
look-elsewhere effect in the three-point case---which triple, which
signs, which running scale---is either inherited from the
established Koide relation or determined by the Descartes
construction.  Table~\ref{tab:ledger} summarizes the accounting.

\begin{table}[htb]
\centering
\caption{Degree-of-freedom ledger for the Descartes/Soddy
  construction, compared with an unconstrained three-point Koide
  search.}
\label{tab:ledger}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.25}
\begin{tabular}{lcc}
\toprule
Aspect & Three-point search & Soddy construction \\
\midrule
Input triple & searched ($\binom{6}{3} = 20$) &
  inherited from Koide \\
Sign pattern & searched ($4$ per triple) &
  inherited (all positive) \\
Evaluation scale(s) & searched (per particle) &
  $\mu_\star = \sum m_\ell$ from Koide \\
Output quantity & $Q \approx 2/3$ (closure) &
  $\mathcal{F} = k_4^-$ (determined) \\
Comparison exponent & --- &
  $n=2$ (dimensional consistency) \\
Target quark & --- &
  1 of 6 (genuine selection) \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\paragraph{Random-spectrum context.}
In the Monte Carlo experiments reported in the main text, the joint
condition---Koide saturation of the input triple plus a Soddy
curvature matching a quark mass within $1\sigma$ at the
triple's own sum-mass scale---appears in fewer than $1\%$ of
retained random samples under all four priors tested.  At the
baseline prior the hit fraction is approximately $0.47\%$, with a
$95\%$ confidence interval from finite sampling.

This number should not be read as a $p$-value.  It is conditional on
the sampling prior, the perturbative cutoff, the scale prescription,
and the filter choices.  A different reference class would give a
different number.  The correct interpretation is simply that, within
the stated toy nulls, the Descartes/Soddy match is rare---and
substantially rarer than a three-point closure under comparable
search freedom.

\paragraph{Interpretation.}
The supplement therefore makes a limited claim.  It does not prove
that $\mathcal{F}^2 = m_s^{\msbar}(\mu_\star)$ is exact, physical,
or non-accidental.  Coincidence remains possible.  Low frequency in
random data means rare, not impossible.

What the calculation does show is that the main observation is not
explained by the same look-elsewhere mechanism that makes
unconstrained three-point running-scale Koide searches
uninformative.  The overfitting cliff appears in the three-point
search when evaluation-scale freedom is admitted.  The
Descartes/Soddy construction sits on the other side of that
comparison: it adds a fourth constrained quantity rather than another
free fitting knob.

In short, the Monte Carlo context supports the following qualitative
distinction:
\[
\text{three-point running-scale closure: common under search
  freedom,}
\]
whereas
\[
\text{four-point Descartes/Soddy match: rare under the stated toy
  nulls.}
\]
This is all the supplement claims.

methodological correctness. For each error or concern, classify its severity.

SEVERITY CLASSIFICATION (required for every finding):

RETRACTION-WORTHY: The error means the paper's central conclusions CANNOT be
supported by the data as presented. Not "could be improved" — "fundamentally broken."
Examples: data coding errors that change the primary outcome, impossible statistical
values, applying tests that don't match the study design rendering all inferences invalid.

MAJOR-REVISION: A real methodological concern that could change conclusions if
addressed differently, but does NOT definitively invalidate them.
Examples: missing controls, underpowered secondary analyses, questionable but
defensible statistical choices.

MINOR: Valid criticism applicable to most published papers. Would not change conclusions.
Examples: no robustness checks, limited generalizability, minor reporting gaps.

KEY TEST: If confirmed, would this need RETRACTION, REVISION, or just NOTING?

findings.finding: The look-elsewhere accounting is incomplete because several elements treated as 'determined' are plausibly post hoc analytical choices rather than pre-specified constraints: the Descartes/Soddy reinterpretation itself, choosing the outer root rather than inner root, comparing F^2 rather than other algebraically natural functions, and selecting the scale mu_* = sum m_l as the comparison scale. The manuscript argues these are forced by construction, but from a discovery-process perspective they appear to be researcher degrees of freedom.
findings.justification: If confirmed, the reported ~0.47% hit rate is likely anticonservative because the null does not include the actual search path that generated the hypothesis. This would not necessarily invalidate the descriptive coincidence, but it could substantially weaken or erase the claim that the match is unusually rare under a fair reference class.

findings.finding: The Monte Carlo null prior over Koide-conditioned triples is ad hoc and not physically justified. Sampling m3 log-uniformly on [10^0,10^4] MeV and m1 log-uniformly on [10^-3,10^-1]·m3, then solving for m2, imposes a very specific geometry on the space of Koide triples. The rarity estimate is therefore highly prior-dependent, and the limited prior-sensitivity check reported does not establish robustness to the much broader class of plausible reference measures.
findings.justification: If confirmed, the paper's quantitative rarity statements could change substantially under alternative parameterizations of Koide-saturating triples. This affects the manuscript’s main inferential support from Monte Carlo, but not the raw numerical observation itself.

findings.finding: The hit criterion appears to use only sigma_{m_s}(mu_*) as tolerance, without propagating uncertainty in the derived quantity F^2 from the input lepton masses and from any scale/running uncertainties. A one-sided error model anchored only to the quark-mass uncertainty is not a symmetric or fully propagated comparison.
findings.justification: If confirmed, the hit count may be misestimated because the acceptance window is defined incompletely. Depending on the relative uncertainties, the reported frequency could increase or decrease. This would require revision of the Monte Carlo result, though likely not retraction.

findings.finding: The manuscript reports exact Monte Carlo counts and a Clopper-Pearson interval, but the simulation is not reproducible from the text: the analytic branch/solution for m2 is not fully specified, criteria for positivity and ordering are not explicit, the running procedure for m_s^MSbar(mu) is underspecified, and the 'audited scan' over quark triples/signs/scales is described only qualitatively. Without full algorithmic detail, the numerical frequencies cannot be independently verified.
findings.justification: If confirmed, the numerical rarity estimates and the comparison to the '50%' unconstrained search may not be auditable. This does not prove they are wrong, but it prevents methodological validation and could alter conclusions once fully specified.

findings.finding: The statement that broadening to all five quark species adds no additional hits is inconsistent with the earlier degree-of-freedom accounting that the target is 'one of six quark species.' The text alternates between six and five targets, apparently excluding top for perturbative/scale reasons without formally integrating that exclusion into the selection accounting.
findings.justification: If confirmed, this is mainly a reporting/consistency problem in multiplicity accounting. It would slightly affect the look-elsewhere discussion but is unlikely by itself to change the paper’s qualitative conclusions.

findings.finding: The comparison between the constrained four-point construction and the 'approximately half of random spectra' in unconstrained three-point searches may not be apples-to-apples. The search spaces, priors, scale lists, and success criteria are different, so the contrast could exaggerate how much rarer the Soddy match is than ordinary Koide closures.
findings.justification: If confirmed, the central interpretive claim that the observation is not explained by the same overfitting mechanism would be weakened because the benchmark comparator is not matched in design. The descriptive observation remains, but the comparative inference would need revision.

findings.finding: Conditioning on retained triples with mu_* > 1 GeV may induce selection effects that are not fully characterized. Since mu_* also defines the evaluation scale, the cutoff is entangled with the outcome assessment rather than being an innocuous pre-analysis filter.
findings.justification: If confirmed, the estimated hit frequency is conditional on a nontrivial truncation tied to the testing scale. Different cutoffs could shift the rarity estimate. This would call for reanalysis but not necessarily invalidate the full paper.

findings.finding: The use of 'dimensional consistency fixes the comparison quantity to F^2' is overstated. While F^2 is the simplest mass-dimension-1 quantity, dimensional analysis alone does not uniquely determine the tested observable in a post hoc numerology setting; dimensionless ratios or normalized functions could also have been considered.
findings.justification: If confirmed, this mainly affects rhetorical strength in the degree-of-freedom ledger. It adds to concerns about hidden analytical flexibility but is not independently fatal.

steps.prompt_sent:
You are an independent scientific reviewer. Review this manuscript for
methodological correctness. For each error or concern, classify its severity.

SEVERITY CLASSIFICATION (required for every finding):

RETRACTION-WORTHY: The error means the paper's central conclusions CANNOT be
supported by the data as presented. Not "could be improved" — "fundamentally broken."
Examples: data coding errors that change the primary outcome, impossible statistical
values, applying tests that don't match the study design rendering all inferences invalid.

MAJOR-REVISION: A real methodological concern that could change conclusions if
addressed differently, but does NOT definitively invalidate them.
Examples: missing controls, underpowered secondary analyses, questionable but
defensible statistical choices.

MINOR: Valid criticism applicable to most published papers. Would not change conclusions.
Examples: no robustness checks, limited generalizability, minor reporting gaps.

KEY TEST: If confirmed, would this need RETRACTION, REVISION, or just NOTING?

Manuscript:
% =====================================================================
% DRAFT: Compressed replacement for Sections 5.4--5.7 and Interpretation
%
% Sections 5.1 (error budget), 5.2 (continuous scale), 5.3 (alternative
% scales) remain unchanged.  Everything below replaces 5.4 onward.
% =====================================================================

\subsection{Degree-of-freedom accounting and null-model context}
\label{sec:dof}

Before reporting Monte Carlo checks, it is useful to state explicitly
which aspects of the comparison were selected by the author and which
were determined by the construction.

\paragraph{Determined quantities (no selection freedom).}
The input triple $(e,\mu,\tau)$ is inherited from the Koide
relation~(\ref{eq:koide}); no search over triples was performed.
Given a Descartes reading of that triple, the two Soddy curvatures
are determined by the three input curvatures; the choice between
inner and outer root is a binary selection (one bit).
The comparison exponent $n=2$ is fixed by dimensional consistency:
$\mathcal{F}$ has units of $\text{MeV}^{1/2}$, so only
$\mathcal{F}^2$ carries mass dimension one.
The evaluation scale $\mu_\star = \sum m_\ell$ is the quantity that
already appears in the numerator of the Koide
relation~(\ref{eq:koide}); it is not an independently tuned parameter.
All signs are positive, again inherited from the established
charged-lepton Koide relation.

\paragraph{Genuine selection freedom.}
The comparison target is one of six quark species; this is the
principal source of look-elsewhere effect.  As shown in
Table~\ref{tab:loose}, among all five light quarks only the strange
quark produces a hit at the stated tolerance, and the charm and
bottom masses are too far from $\mathcal{F}^2 \approx 95$~MeV at
any perturbative scale for the comparison to be close.

\paragraph{Contrast with unconstrained Koide searches.}
Three-point Koide closures of the form $Q(m_1, m_2, m_3) \approx 2/3$
become common once one admits a search over quark triples
($\binom{6}{3} = 20$ candidates), sign assignments ($\pm$ on each
square root, four patterns), and running evaluation scales (multiple
physically motivated scales per quark).  In an audited scan of the
Standard Model quark spectrum over this full search space---20
triples $\times$ 4 sign patterns $\times$ a discrete list of
physically interpretable evaluation scales per
quark---approximately half of all random spectra produce at least one
close three-point closure.  The mechanism is straightforward: each
additional search knob multiplies the number of independent trials,
and the accumulated look-elsewhere effect overwhelms any individual
match.  The Descartes/Soddy construction moves in the opposite
direction: it takes one established triple, determines a fourth
quantity from it, and inherits its scale from the same expression.

\paragraph{Null-model Monte Carlo.}
To quantify the rarity of the joint condition (Koide saturation plus
Soddy--quark match), we sample Koide-conditioned triples as follows.
Draw $m_3$ log-uniformly from $[10^0,10^4]$~MeV and $m_1$
log-uniformly from $[10^{-3},10^{-1}]\cdot m_3$; solve $m_2$
analytically from $Q(m_1,m_2,m_3) = 2/3$.  Retain only triples with
$\mu_\star > 1$~GeV (perturbative running regime), accumulating
$25{,}485$ valid triples.  For each, compute $\mathcal{F}^{(i)}$ and
$\mu_\star^{(i)}$, run $m_s^{\msbar}(\mu_\star^{(i)})$ from the
FLAG 2~GeV input, and record a hit if
$|\mathcal{F}^{(i)\,2} - m_s^{\msbar}(\mu_\star^{(i)})| <
\sigma_{m_s}(\mu_\star^{(i)})$.  Of the retained triples, $121$
produce hits:
\begin{equation}
p_{\text{tight}} = 0.47\%,\qquad
95\%\;\text{Clopper--Pearson CI:}\;[0.39\%,\,0.57\%].
\label{eq:tight_null_v2}
\end{equation}
Broadening the comparison to all five quark species adds no
additional hits (Table~\ref{tab:loose}): charm, bottom, up, and down
masses are too far from $\mathcal{F}^2$ at any perturbative scale.
The hit fraction is stable across four prior choices (range
$0.23\%$--$0.47\%$, fold variation $2.0$; see
Table~\ref{tab:priors}) and three nontrivial lower-cutoff choices
(range $0.41\%$--$0.58\%$, fold variation $1.4$).

These are conditional frequencies under specific Monte Carlo
constructions, not $p$-values.  Their purpose is limited: they
confirm that the Descartes/Soddy match is not explained by the same
look-elsewhere mechanism that makes unconstrained three-point
running-scale Koide searches uninformative.  We draw no further
inferential conclusion from them.


% =====================================================================
% SUPPLEMENTARY NOTE: Look-Elsewhere Context
% (standalone supplement, can be appended or included separately)
% =====================================================================

\newpage
\section*{Supplementary Note: Look-Elsewhere Context}

The main text reports a post-hoc numerical observation:
\[
\mathcal{F}^2 = 95.113~\text{MeV}
\;\approx\;
m_s^{\msbar}(\mu_\star),
\qquad
\mu_\star = m_e + m_\mu + m_\tau.
\]
Because Koide-type mass relations are vulnerable to look-elsewhere
effects, we include here a limited Monte Carlo context calculation.
The purpose is not to assign an objective probability to the
observation.  The purpose is narrower: to compare the observed
Descartes/Soddy construction with the known failure mode of
three-point Koide searches, namely the inflation of apparent
significance when running-scale freedom is admitted.

\paragraph{Three-point Koide searches.}
For a three-mass Koide expression
\[
Q(m_1,m_2,m_3)
= \frac{m_1 + m_2 + m_3}
{\bigl(s_1\sqrt{m_1} + s_2\sqrt{m_2} + s_3\sqrt{m_3}\bigr)^2},
\qquad s_i \in \{\pm 1\},
\]
close numerical closures become common once one allows a search over
triples, signs, and running evaluation scales.  In an audited
quark-only scan over $20$ triples, four sign assignments, and a
discrete list of physically interpretable evaluation scales, the
probability of at least one close three-point closure in a random
spectrum approaches $50\%$.  This is the overfitting cliff: once the
running scale becomes a search knob, a close three-point closure is
no longer surprising.

The cliff is visible even in a controlled ablation.  Starting from
a single constrained triple evaluated at a single physically
motivated scale (hit rate ${\sim}0.5\%$), each additional degree of
freedom---admitting sign flips, admitting per-particle scale
choices---raises the hit rate monotonically.  By the time all three
particles in a triple are free to run independently, the hit rate
exceeds that of the constrained starting point by an order of
magnitude.  When the search is broadened to all $20$ quark triples
with full sign and scale freedom, approximately half of random
spectra contain at least one close closure.

This observation is not used as evidence against every possible quark
Koide relation.  It only says that broad three-point running-scale
searches are insufficiently constrained to be informative.

\paragraph{The four-point Descartes/Soddy construction.}
The construction in the main text is different.  Once the
charged-lepton Koide triple is interpreted through the ordinary
Descartes circle theorem, the fourth curvature is not freely chosen.
The two Descartes roots are fixed by the three input curvatures
$k_i = \sqrt{m_i}$.  Choosing the outer root gives
\[
\mathcal{F} = k_4^- = e_1 - 2\sqrt{e_2},
\]
and dimensional consistency fixes the comparison quantity to
$\mathcal{F}^2$.  The evaluation scale $\mu_\star = \sum m_\ell$ is
the quantity already present in the Koide numerator, not an
independently tuned parameter.  The remaining numerical comparison
is then whether this derived fourth mass-scale quantity lies near a
known quark mass at a stated scale.

Thus the Descartes/Soddy comparison is a four-point constraint
rather than a three-point closure.  Each element that inflates the
look-elsewhere effect in the three-point case---which triple, which
signs, which running scale---is either inherited from the
established Koide relation or determined by the Descartes
construction.  Table~\ref{tab:ledger} summarizes the accounting.

\begin{table}[htb]
\centering
\caption{Degree-of-freedom ledger for the Descartes/Soddy
  construction, compared with an unconstrained three-point Koide
  search.}
\label{tab:ledger}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.25}
\begin{tabular}{lcc}
\toprule
Aspect & Three-point search & Soddy construction \\
\midrule
Input triple & searched ($\binom{6}{3} = 20$) &
  inherited from Koide \\
Sign pattern & searched ($4$ per triple) &
  inherited (all positive) \\
Evaluation scale(s) & searched (per particle) &
  $\mu_\star = \sum m_\ell$ from Koide \\
Output quantity & $Q \approx 2/3$ (closure) &
  $\mathcal{F} = k_4^-$ (determined) \\
Comparison exponent & --- &
  $n=2$ (dimensional consistency) \\
Target quark & --- &
  1 of 6 (genuine selection) \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\paragraph{Random-spectrum context.}
In the Monte Carlo experiments reported in the main text, the joint
condition---Koide saturation of the input triple plus a Soddy
curvature matching a quark mass within $1\sigma$ at the
triple's own sum-mass scale---appears in fewer than $1\%$ of
retained random samples under all four priors tested.  At the
baseline prior the hit fraction is approximately $0.47\%$, with a
$95\%$ confidence interval from finite sampling.

This number should not be read as a $p$-value.  It is conditional on
the sampling prior, the perturbative cutoff, the scale prescription,
and the filter choices.  A different reference class would give a
different number.  The correct interpretation is simply that, within
the stated toy nulls, the Descartes/Soddy match is rare---and
substantially rarer than a three-point closure under comparable
search freedom.

\paragraph{Interpretation.}
The supplement therefore makes a limited claim.  It does not prove
that $\mathcal{F}^2 = m_s^{\msbar}(\mu_\star)$ is exact, physical,
or non-accidental.  Coincidence remains possible.  Low frequency in
random data means rare, not impossible.

What the calculation does show is that the main observation is not
explained by the same look-elsewhere mechanism that makes
unconstrained three-point running-scale Koide searches
uninformative.  The overfitting cliff appears in the three-point
search when evaluation-scale freedom is admitted.  The
Descartes/Soddy construction sits on the other side of that
comparison: it adds a fourth constrained quantity rather than another
free fitting knob.

In short, the Monte Carlo context supports the following qualitative
distinction:
\[
\text{three-point running-scale closure: common under search
  freedom,}
\]
whereas
\[
\text{four-point Descartes/Soddy match: rare under the stated toy
  nulls.}
\]
This is all the supplement claims.

      "justification": "what breaks if confirmed?"}
  ]}

parsed_response.reasoning: The manuscript presents a novel mathematical construction linking lepton and quark masses but contains severe methodological flaws in its statistical validation. The core issue is the misuse of Monte Carlo simulations to create an illusion of significance while explicitly disclaiming their inferential value. This creates a fundamental contradiction: the authors rely on the low hit rate (0.47%) as evidence that their finding is 'rare' and not explained by look-elsewhere effects, yet simultaneously state these are 'not p-values' and draw 'no further inferential conclusion.' This dual stance invalidates the paper's central claim of providing meaningful context. The methodological framework is internally inconsistent and does not support the conclusions drawn.

findings.finding: Contradictory use of Monte Carlo results. The authors compute a hit rate (0.47%) with a confidence interval, explicitly label it as 'not a p-value,' and state they draw 'no further inferential conclusion.' However, the entire 'Interpretation' and 'Supplementary Note' sections are built upon the rhetorical weight of this low frequency ('rare under the stated toy nulls') to argue their finding is noteworthy and distinct from common Koide closures. This is a logical fallacy: using a statistic as evidence while disclaiming its evidential value.
findings.justification: If confirmed, this methodological incoherence means the paper's central argument—that the Descartes/Soddy match is meaningfully rare and thus interesting—collapses. The authors cannot have it both ways. Either the Monte Carlo provides relevant statistical context (in which case proper hypothesis testing and multiple testing corrections are needed), or it does not (in which case reporting it with a CI is misleading). The current presentation is fundamentally unsound for supporting scientific conclusions.

findings.finding: The 'null model' is not a null model. The Monte Carlo samples triples that are constrained to exactly satisfy the Koide relation (Q=2/3). This conditions on the very phenomenon being investigated (the Koide relation for leptons) and then checks for a secondary coincidence. A true null model for assessing the rarity of the joint event (Koide + mass match) would sample triples from a distribution representing the 'null' of no special relation (e.g., random masses within physical ranges) and then apply both the Koide filter and the Soddy-quark check. The described procedure only estimates the conditional probability of the quark match GIVEN the Koide relation, which is not the relevant probability for assessing the overall surprise of the finding.
findings.justification: This error invalidates the quantitative interpretation of the 0.47% figure. The probability that a random, Koide-satisfying triple also yields a Soddy curvature near a quark mass is not the probability of observing the entire phenomenon by chance. The appropriate null would be the chance of finding *both* a Koide-like triple AND the quark match from a spectrum with no pre-imposed relations. The current setup artificially deflates the hit rate by preselecting for one unusual property (Koide saturation), making the subsequent coincidence seem rarer than it is in a fully random search.

findings.finding: Incomplete control for the 'principal source of look-elsewhere effect.' The authors correctly identify the choice of target quark (1 of 6) as a key selection degree of freedom but then claim 'broadening the comparison to all five quark species adds no additional hits.' This is a post-hoc observation that does not correct for the selection. The proper analysis must account for the fact that they looked at 6 quarks. The hit rate calculation should be: what fraction of Monte Carlo trials yield a hit with *any* of the 5 light quarks? The statement that others are 'too far' is a result of their specific draw, not a correction. The probability of a match with *any* quark is inherently higher than with just the strange quark.
findings.justification: If corrected, this would materially increase the observed 'hit fraction' in the Monte Carlo, weakening the claim of rarity. Failing to account for the multiple comparisons across quark species, even if only one yields a hit in this instance, is a standard multiple testing error. The inference is based on a selected outcome without penalizing the selection process.

findings.finding: The parameter ranges and prior (log-uniform) for the Monte Carlo are arbitrary and influential. The hit rate is shown to vary by a factor of ~2 based on prior choice. The choice of a log-uniform prior over specific decades ([10^0,10^4] MeV for m3) is not justified by a physical null model. Mass spectra might follow different distributions (e.g., roughly geometric). The result's sensitivity to these arbitrary choices undermines any quantitative claim, even the conditional one the authors attempt to make.
findings.justification: While this is a common issue in exploratory theoretical work and wouldn't alone invalidate the core idea, it highlights the fragility of the quantitative context provided. It means the specific 0.47% number is not robust, which is acceptable only if the authors strictly treat it as a toy demonstration, not as supportive evidence.

findings.finding: The manuscript blurs the line between a post-hoc observation and a predictive test. The entire exercise is presented as assessing a 'post-hoc numerical observation.' The methodology does not constitute a test of a pre-registered hypothesis. The careful 'degree-of-freedom accounting' is an attempt to argue the finding is not a product of excessive searching, but the statistical framework does not support a valid inference from the observed data.
findings.justification: This is a fundamental limitation of the study design common in this field. It doesn't render the mathematical observation incorrect, but it strictly limits the strength of any concluding statement. The paper's conclusions should be framed as a curious coincidence suggesting a direction for future theoretical work, not as evidence supported by statistical validation.

In the harshest reading, the reported rarity is structurally deflated because the simulation preselects an already special class.

If the target species was selected after seeing the data, then the relevant Monte Carlo question is the probability of matching any admissible target under the same search rule, not whether in the observed dataset only one target happened to succeed. Their point is strongest if the manuscript presents the strange-quark comparison as effectively singled out by theory only after several choices that were themselves informed by the observation.

On severity, the best argument for going harder than I did is this: if the Monte Carlo is conceptually misused, and if the paper’s main interpretive conclusion is that the coincidence is meaningfully rare in a way that distinguishes it from generic numerology, then the inferential core may indeed be unsupported as presented, not just in need of calibration. In that framing, some issues I called MAJOR-REVISION could be seen as approaching RETRACTION-WORTHY for the current version’s main takeaway, even if the descriptive numerical coincidence survives.

That said, even on the strongest steelman, one must separate two claims: (1) the descriptive observation exists; (2) the paper has established a sound statistical context making it notably rare. The other reviewer’s harshest position is strongest against (2), not (1).
severity_i_now_upgrade: Possible upgrade: 'Contradictory use of Monte Carlo results' from implicit concern within my reasoning to a standalone MAJOR-REVISION finding. I did not isolate this conceptual incoherence sharply enough. If the manuscript truly relies on the 0.47% figure to support its central interpretation while disclaiming evidential use, that deserves explicit major criticism.

Possible upgrade: my finding on incomplete look-elsewhere accounting could arguably be stated even more severely within MAJOR-REVISION, because if the discovery path was substantially post hoc then the reported rarity may not just be somewhat anticonservative but qualitatively misleading.

Possible upgrade: the species-multiplicity issue from MINOR to MAJOR-REVISION if, on rereading, the strange-quark target was not genuinely pre-specified and the simulation did not evaluate 'any admissible quark target' under the same search protocol. In that case it is not just a wording inconsistency but a real multiple-testing defect.
severity_i_now_downgrade: I would likely downgrade the claim that conditioning on exact Koide-satisfying triples makes the Monte Carlo 'not a null model' for the paper’s stated question, if the authors are careful that they are estimating only a conditional rarity given Koide saturation. I still think the manuscript risks overinterpreting that conditional quantity, but the conditional design itself is not automatically erroneous. So I would not upgrade this to RETRACTION-WORTHY and might resist treating it as categorically invalid.

I would resist any move from my MAJOR-REVISION findings to RETRACTION-WORTHY on the basis of prior sensitivity alone. The ad hoc prior is a serious weakness, but toy/reference-class simulations with arbitrary priors can still be acceptable if transparently presented as exploratory and non-decisive.
findings_i_still_defend: Incomplete look-elsewhere accounting — MAJOR-REVISION. I still stand by this. The analytical path appears to contain more researcher degrees of freedom than the manuscript credits, and that can materially alter rarity estimates.
Ad hoc null prior over Koide-conditioned triples — MAJOR-REVISION. I still defend this severity. The result is strongly prior-dependent and not grounded in a compelling physical reference measure.

Uncertainty propagation / hit criterion incompleteness — MAJOR-REVISION. I still defend this. The acceptance window should reflect uncertainties in both compared quantities and any running inputs, not only the quark-mass uncertainty.

Insufficient reproducibility/auditability of the Monte Carlo and running procedure — MAJOR-REVISION. I strongly defend this. Without full algorithmic specification, the exact frequency claims cannot be independently checked.

Constrained-vs-unconstrained comparison not apples-to-apples — MAJOR-REVISION. I still defend this because the manuscript uses this contrast interpretively, and mismatched search spaces/priors can distort the comparison.
mu_* > 1 GeV conditioning entangled with the evaluation scale — MAJOR-REVISION. I still think this is a real methodological issue that could affect frequencies.
'F^2 is uniquely fixed by dimensional consistency' overstated — MINOR. I still think this is mainly rhetorical overreach unless it is doing substantial hidden work in the multiplicity ledger.
'five vs six quark species' reporting inconsistency — MINOR unless the species choice was truly post hoc and unaccounted for in simulation, in which case I would reconsider upward.
new_errors_noticed: A fresh issue is potential category confusion between descriptive Monte Carlo calibration and evidential inference. Even if the authors avoid the term p-value, reporting exact hit rates with confidence intervals may still invite inferential reading unless the manuscript sharply limits what can be concluded. This should be explicitly examined as a presentation-level methodological problem.

Another fresh issue is that if the same observed data informed both the construction choices and the tolerance/target framing, then the Monte Carlo may be double-conditioned on data-dependent choices in a way not reflected anywhere in the reference class.

The manuscript may need a clearer distinction between 'conditional rarity within a hand-built family' and 'global surprise of the observed phenomenon.' Conflating those is a substantive interpretive error even if every simulation step is coded correctly.

If the 'audited scan' over signs/roots/scales was conducted after noticing the coincidence, then that scan itself becomes part of the search process and should be reflected in multiplicity accounting; my original review noted this piecemeal but perhaps not as an integrated end-to-end protocol problem.

The reported hit rate is sensitive to prior choice (varying by ~2× in limited checks), and the broader class of plausible reference measures has not been explored.
findings.justification: Both reviewers flagged this. Review B rated it MINOR; Review A rated it MAJOR-REVISION. After steelman exchange, the consensus is that for a paper whose central quantitative claim is a specific rarity percentage, factor-of-2 sensitivity to an unjustified prior is a substantive concern warranting revision, not merely a minor caveat.

findings.finding: Incomplete multiplicity correction across quark species. The paper identifies the target quark (1 of 6) as the principal look-elsewhere source, then argues post hoc that no other quarks yield hits. The proper Monte Carlo should evaluate the fraction of trials matching any admissible quark, not just the strange quark. The inconsistency between '6 quarks' and '5 quarks' (excluding top) further clouds the accounting.
findings.justification: Review B rated this MAJOR-REVISION; Review A initially split it into a MINOR reporting inconsistency and a broader look-elsewhere concern but acknowledged during steelman that if the species choice was post hoc, it merits MAJOR-REVISION. The proper correction could materially increase the effective hit rate.

findings.finding: The hit criterion uses only the quark-mass uncertainty σ_{m_s}(μ*) as tolerance, without propagating uncertainty in the derived quantity F² from input lepton masses, the Koide solution, or running/scale uncertainties. The acceptance window is therefore incomplete.
findings.justification: Raised by Review A; not contested by Review B during steelman. Depending on relative magnitudes, the acceptance window could be significantly under- or overestimated, materially affecting the hit count.

findings.finding: Insufficient algorithmic specification for reproducibility. The analytic branch/solution for m₂ given the Koide constraint, positivity and ordering criteria, the running procedure for m_s^{MSbar}(μ), and the 'audited scan' protocol are not fully specified. The numerical frequencies cannot be independently verified from the manuscript text.
findings.justification: Raised by Review A; recognized as important by Review B during steelman exchange. For a paper whose interpretive weight rests on a specific Monte Carlo frequency, non-reproducibility is a substantive methodological deficiency.

findings.finding: The comparison between the constrained four-point Soddy construction (~0.47% hit rate) and unconstrained three-point Koide searches ('~50% of random spectra') uses different search spaces, priors, scale lists, and success criteria. The contrast may exaggerate how much rarer the Soddy match is relative to ordinary Koide closures.
findings.justification: Raised by Review A. Not directly contested during steelman. The manuscript uses this comparison as a key interpretive argument; if the benchmark is not matched in design, the comparative conclusion is weakened.

findings.finding: Conditioning on retained triples with μ* > 1 GeV may induce selection effects entangled with the outcome assessment, since μ* also defines the scale at which the quark mass is evaluated.
findings.justification: Raised by Review A; acknowledged as valid by Review B during steelman. The entanglement is subtle but could bias the hit-rate estimate in a direction that is not characterized.

findings.finding: The claim that 'dimensional consistency fixes the comparison quantity to F²' is overstated. While F² is the simplest mass-dimension-1 quantity, dimensional analysis does not uniquely determine the tested observable; dimensionless ratios or other functions could also have been considered.
findings.justification: Both reviewers treated this as minor. It adds to the aggregate concern about hidden analytical flexibility but does not independently change conclusions.

findings.finding: The paper is fundamentally a post-hoc observation rather than a pre-registered predictive test. The 'degree-of-freedom accounting' attempts to bound the search space but cannot fully substitute for pre-specification.
findings.justification: Both reviewers note this as an inherent limitation. It constrains the maximum interpretive weight of any statistical context the paper provides but is common in exploratory theoretical work and does not by itself invalidate the contribution.
